title: Feature-Market Fit: General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

sections.title: Step 09 — Technology-Level Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
**Technology class:** Emergency Exit Security Devices with Remote Monitoring  
**Commodity FP:** Prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability and alerting to door activation via audible alarm  
**Market job:** Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus

### Top 10 Market Needs (grounding basis)

| # | Statement (short) | Imp | Sat | OS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N08 | Undetected unauthorized activation >5 min, overnight/weekend | 10 | 2 | **18** |
| N11 | Battery status monitoring for 200+ door campus, single dashboard | 9 | 2 | **16** |
| N16 | JCI-compliant 12-month log generation without manual aggregation | 9 | 2 | **16** |
| N12 | RF transmission failures from MRI/CT interference | 9 | 4 | **14** |
| N20 | Compliance gap visibility during unannounced JCI survey | 9 | 4 | **14** |
| N13 | Correlate exit events with elopement incidents <30 sec timestamp | 8 | 3 | 13 |
| N19 | On-demand real-time compliance status for surveyors | 8 | 3 | 13 |
| N04 | Installation time <30 min/door, no electrician, in occupied wards | 8 | 5 | 11 |
| N09 | False alarm frequency >2 events/week on high-traffic exits | 8 | 4 | 12 |
| N21 | Alert-to-dispatch <15 sec, location-specific | 7 | 2 | 12 |

### Feature Kano Classification & ODI Scores

| Feature | Kano | Time | Cost | Safety | Reli | Skill | Stress | Pain | Conf | Del | **Overall** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Alarm Trigger | must_be | 3 | 4 | 9 | 9 | 7 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 2 | **6.11** |
| Key Override Mechanism | must_be | 7 | 4 | 8 | 8 | 6 | 8 | 7 | 7 | 3 | **6.44** |
| Pre-Alarm Warning Tone | attractive | 5 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 7 | 8 | 8 | 6 | 6 | **6.11** |
| Wireless State Transmitter | one_dimensional | 7 | 6 | 8 | 6 | 5 | 8 | 7 | 7 | 6 | **6.67** |
| Battery Power Supply (no mains) | must_be | 3 | 7 | 8 | 8 | 7 | 5 | 7 | 8 | 3 | **6.22** |
| Remote Battery Status Reporting | attractive | 6 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 8 | 8 | 8 | **7.33** |
| Retrofit Surface Mount Installation | one_dimensional | 8 | 9 | 5 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 9 | 6 | 7 | **7.22** |
| Automated Event Log Generation | attractive | 7 | 8 | 6 | 7 | 7 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 7 | **7.67** |

### Rationale by Feature

**Mechanical Alarm Trigger** (`must_be`, 6.11)  
Any emergency exit product in a hospital must provide an audible alarm — NFPA 101 §18 and DIN EN 179/1125 make this non-negotiable. Presence is taken for granted; no delight. Primary drivers: Safety (9) — fail-safe at 0V is critical for elopement detection during power events; Reliability (9) — mechanical linkage eliminates electrical single-point-of-failure. N08 (undetected activation, OS=18) is directly addressed by this feature.

**Key Override Mechanism** (`must_be`, 6.44)  
Hospitals generate legitimate clinical emergencies requiring rapid authorized exit — code blue, fire evacuation drills, psychiatric handoffs. A key bypass that suppresses the alarm is expected infrastructure. Primary drivers: Stress (8) — eliminates nurse/security anxiety during authorized clinical access; Safety (8) — prevents inappropriate escalation during acute events. N10 (authorized bypass <10 sec, no facility-wide alarm) is directly addressed.

**Pre-Alarm Warning Tone** (`attractive`, 6.11)  
Not present on all competing alternatives. Clinical settings have high incidental door-contact rates (staff rushing through). A warning tone that aborts false alarms before full activation is genuinely surprising in this category. Primary drivers: Pain (8) — eliminates false alarm call-outs in busy wards (N09, false alarm rate); Stress (8) — reduces staff desensitization to alarms, which is a documented patient-safety risk.

**Wireless State Transmitter** (`one_dimensional`, 6.67)  
More reliable, wider coverage = more value. Directly addresses the #1 underserved need (N08, OS=18: undetected activation during overnight). Primary drivers: Safety (8) — real-time transmission closes the detection gap that manual patrol cannot; Stress (8) — overnight staff confidence in remote visibility. Constraint C3 (30m range limit in reinforced hospital construction) and C6 (EasyWave not native to BACnet nurse-call systems) cap the Reliability score at 6. Linear: more range, better scores.

**Battery Power Supply (no mains)** (`must_be`, 6.22)  
Retrofit in occupied hospital wards without electrician access is a practical necessity. No mains at door is expected for this product class. Primary drivers: Reliability (8) — alarm uptime during power failure events (C2 satisfied); Cost (7) — zero electrician spend across 200+ door retrofit (N04); Confidence (8) — power-independent operation is certifiable. Delight is low (3) — buyers expect battery power, they do not celebrate it.

**Remote Battery Status Reporting** (`attractive`, 7.33)  
Best score in the market. Manual battery checking across 200+ doors is a documented operational burden (N11, OS=16). Competing mechanical alternatives have zero remote battery visibility. Primary drivers: Pain (8) — eliminates manual maintenance rounds (N15); Stress (8) — eliminates the anxiety of discovering dead batteries after an elopement incident; Confidence (8) — proactive replacement before failure achievable; Delight (8) — facilities directors genuinely surprised by this capability in a door alarm product.

**Retrofit Surface Mount Installation** (`one_dimensional`, 7.22)  
More retrofit simplicity = more value. Wired alternatives require 2–4 hrs/door with licensed electricians in occupied wards; this product takes 15–30 min with no construction work. Directly addresses N04 (installation <30 min, no electrician). Primary drivers: Skill (9) — facility tech replaces specialized trades; Pain (9) — eliminates the dominant retrofit barrier in occupied hospitals; Cost (9) — no electrician, no conduit, no permit, no disruption to clinical operations. Reliability lower (5) because surface mount creates tamper exposure in high-traffic clinical corridors.

**Automated Event Log Generation** (`attractive`, 7.67)  
Highest scoring feature. JCI compliance documentation is a critical and chronically underserved need (N16, OS=16; N20, OS=14). No competing mechanical alternative provides timestamped event logs. Primary drivers: Confidence (9) — 12-month audit trail available on demand for surveyors (N19); Stress (9) — eliminates pre-survey documentation scramble (N20); Pain (9) — replaces manual log compilation for JCI Environment of Care reports (N16). Attractive because it adds a genuinely unexpected capability relative to the commodity FP of "audible alarm."

---

sections.title: Step 10 — Product-Related Needs (Burleson Product Jobs)
sections.content:
Needs that arise **because** of the spring-loaded mechanical guard + 868 MHz EasyWave architecture. Would disappear if replaced by wired alarmed exit bars, electronic access control, or any non-wireless alternative.

| # | Statement | Source Constraint | Affected Markets | Imp | Sat |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PR01 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless signal attenuation below reliable reporting threshold caused by reinforced-concrete hospital floor construction exceeding 3 barriers between device and receiver | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 8 | 4 |
| PR02 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation in hospital buildings requiring unplanned repeater infrastructure additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 8 | 4 |
| PR03 | Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures caused by RF interference generated by hospital MRI and CT imaging equipment operating in zones adjacent to monitored exits | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 9 | 4 |
| PR04 | Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations or patient care in adjacent rooms | IoT Battery Life Floor (C4) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 7 | 5 |
| PR05 | Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware configurations — glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, or legacy panic bars — present in pre-1990 hospital wings | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount (C5) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 7 | 5 |
| PR06 | Minimize the likelihood of door event logs transmitted via the wireless monitoring system containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established processing basis under GDPR EU 2016/679 | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance (C8) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 8 | 4 |
| PR07 | Minimize the integration effort required to relay wireless alarm events from the EasyWave protocol into hospital BACnet or nurse-call BMS platforms without a custom protocol gateway | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In (C6) | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals | 7 | 3 |

**Litmus-test verification:**  
- PR01–03 disappear with wired alarmed exit bars → pass  
- PR04 disappears with wired or mains-powered alternatives → pass  
- PR05 disappears with flush-integrated electronic systems → pass  
- PR06 disappears if event data is not transmitted digitally (mechanical-only alarm) → pass  
- PR07 disappears with native BACnet/KNX exit control systems → pass

---

sections.title: Fit Summary
sections.content:
| Dimension | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Strongest fit** | Automated Event Log Generation (7.67), Remote Battery Status Reporting (7.33), Retrofit Surface Mount (7.22) — all address top-3 underserved needs directly |
| **Market must-have** | Mechanical Alarm Trigger, Key Override, Battery Power — must_be baseline; absence = market rejection |
| **Competitive gap** | Remote Battery Status Reporting and Automated Event Log Generation are `attractive` features absent from all mechanical-only competitors; these are primary switching arguments |
| **Constraint-driven risk** | C3 (range in hospital concrete) and C6 (EasyWave/BACnet incompatibility) are primary deployment friction points for this market (PR01–03, PR07) |
| **Overall market fit** | **Strong.** Five of eight features score ≥6.22. The three attractive features directly address the top underserved ODI needs (OS=16–18). Hospital-specific liabilities (RF interference, GDPR, BACnet integration) are known and mitigable. |

---
